metadata.title: Supplemental Material for An Empirical Taxonomy of Leadership Situations: Development, Validation, and Implications for the Science and Practice of Leadership

abstract: The situation plays an important role in leadership, yet there exists no comprehensive, well-accepted, and empirically validated framework for modeling leadership situations. This research used situation ratings and narratives from 1,159 leaders to empirically develop a taxonomy of leadership situations. Natural language processing techniques were used to generate psychological situation characteristics that were then rated by leaders. Factor analyses of leader ratings resulted in a taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics with six dimensions (Positive Uniqueness, Importance, Negativity, Scope, Typicality, and Ease). Topic modeling of leader narratives provided a preliminary accompanying typology of structural leadership situation cue combinations (Market/Business Needs, Barriers to Effectiveness, Interpersonal Resources, Deviations/Changes, Team Objectives, and Logistics). To facilitate the measurement of the perceptions of situations, we developed a 27-item measure of the six dimensions of psychological leadership situation characteristics: the Leadership Situation Questionnaire (LSQ). We used the LSQ to conduct initial tests of the nomological network of psychological leadership situation characteristics by assessing their relationships with leader personality, leader behavior, outcomes of leadership situations, and structural leadership situation cue combinations. The psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy and the resulting measure (the LSQ) provide an organizing framework for existing leadership research, lay a foundation for future research on situation-related leadership hypotheses, and offer important practical implications in areas such as leader assessment and development.

paragraphs: The situation is recognized as important for leadership, but existing treatments of situations in leadership research have been widely acknowledged to be incomplete, idiosyncratic, noncomparable, and more generally in need of improvement (Conger, 2004;Graeff, 1997;Yukl, 2011;Zaccaro et al., 2018). As a result, our ability to study leaders in situ-for the purposes of refining and testing leadership theory as well as assessing, training, and developing leaders-is necessarily limited. Future research would be aided considerably by a taxonomy of leadership situations that provides a common framework of situations as leaders interpret them, which in turn is important to properly (vs. idiosyncratically, as in the present empirical literature) test fundamental hypotheses regarding the consequences (and antecedents) of leadership situations.

Unfortunately, existing taxonomies cannot fully satisfy these requirements. Existing taxonomies of situations in general, though well-developed, are not contextualized to leadership situations specifically. Moreover, two primary existing leadership situation taxonomies (Hoffman & Lord, 2013;Oc, 2018), though useful, are top-down and conceptual (vs. empirical), and, as we discuss subsequently, approach the issue from particular perspectives that limit their ability to properly represent the domain of psychological leadership situation characteristics (i.e., situations as leaders interpret them). Both general situation taxonomies (when applied to leadership situations specifically) and existing top-down, conceptual leadership situation taxonomies may exclude important characteristics of leadership situations and/or include irrelevant characteristics. An empirically derived taxonomy is best suited to uncover all (or most) dimensions of situations related to leadership because it uses a bottom-up approach to derive these dimensions from a comprehensive range of situations encountered by leaders.

In the present article, we describe the development, validation, and intended uses of a comprehensive taxonomy of psychological characteristics (vs. more objective structural aspects) of leadership situations. Our empirical approach to develop the taxonomy did not employ a particular conceptual "lens"; instead, we used the lexical approach, which aims to identify all the important linguistic distinctions that exist in the domain of interest, to reveal the adjectives that define the domain of psychological leadership situation characteristics. Subsequently, we developed the Leadership Situation Questionnaire (LSQ), a 27-item self-report survey measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics.

Additionally, we provide a conceptual model (i.e., nomological network) of psychological leadership situation characteristics, and we conducted preliminary empirical tests of the model. As a part of these empirical tests, we assessed the relationships between psychological leadership situation characteristics and (more objective) structural leadership situation types. To facilitate this, we developed an empirically based typology of structural leadership situation cue combinations. However, unlike the psychological leadership situation characteristics (for which we additionally developed a survey measure-the LSQ-from which scores were derived), we derived scores on the structural leadership situation cue combinations via machine learning-in particular, via an approach known as topic modeling-which we applied to situational narratives provided to us by leaders. We also empirically examined the relationships of the psychological leadership situation characteristics with leader behavior, leadership outcomes, and leader personality. After describing these efforts, we end the present article with an agenda for future research and implications for practice. In sum, the present study provides a basis for a comprehensive understanding of leadership situations and, in turn, a foundation for the future study and shaping of leadership in situ.

sections.header_raw: How Situations Have Been Studied in Leadership Research
sections.header_norm: how situations have been studied in leadership research
paragraphs: The situation has been an indelible aspect of leadership since Fielder's contingency theory. Fiedler's (1964Fiedler's ( , 1978) ) theory described how particular situational elements moderated the influence of trait-like behavioral orientations on leader effectiveness. Later, models such as the situational leadership model (Hersey et al., 1979), path goal theory (House, 1971(House, , 1996)), and the leader substitutes model (Howell et al., 1990;Kerr & Jermier, 1978) elaborated on ways that the leader's situation influenced the effectiveness of particular leadership behaviors or even the need for leadership itself. The focus on the situation as an important influence on leadership has continued in recent frameworks of leader traits and behaviors (Dinh & Lord, 2012;Zaccaro et al., 2018). In general, leadership research has posited that situational characteristics can exhibit main effects on leader behavior (e.g., Dinh & Lord, 2012;Vroom & Yetton, 1973) and leadership outcomes (e.g., Kerr & Jermier, 1978) and can moderate the relationships between leader behavior and leadership outcomes (e.g., House & Mitchell, 1975) and the relationships between leader traits and leadership outcomes (e.g., Fiedler, 1964).

Despite this strong and enduring emphasis on the situation in leadership theory, insufficient attention has been paid to the fundamental situational dimensions that offer explanatory and moderating mechanisms of leadership dynamics. Most prior frameworks specify overly broad situational categories or idiosyncratic situational exemplars. For example, the situational characteristics are not comparable across Fiedler's (1967Fiedler's ( , 1978) ) theory, Hersey's et al. (1979) situational leadership theory, and leadership substitutes theory (Howell et al., 1990;Kerr & Jermier, 1978). Most of the situational variables across these influential models are either broad categories of contextual parameters (e.g., task, follower) or specific and idiosyncratic characteristics (e.g., follower maturity). Broad classifications are too global to make meaningful and more nuanced conceptual distinctions among discrete categories of situational influences on leadership. Alternatively, overly narrow specifications of situational attributes inhibit the generalization of models and findings across meaningfully similar contexts. Moreover, across prior situational leadership frameworks, there is little reference to the core dimensions of the subjective (i.e., psychological) experience of the situation. Collectively, leadership research has articulated a plethora of labels for situational concepts (thereby exhibiting the "jingle" and "jangle" fallacies, wherein situational concepts referred to via the same label may be different and vice versa). Accordingly, despite their ubiquity in leadership research, situational and contingency models have not fared well in terms of empirical evidence and support (DeRue et al., 2011;Graeff, 1997;Papworth et al., 2009;Vecchio, 1983;Wofford & Liska, 1993).

In the meantime, models of leadership behaviors (e.g., transformational, charismatic, ethical leadership styles) have proliferated (e.g., Bowers & Seashore, 1966;Fleishman, 1953;Hemphill & Coons, 1957;Klein & House, 1995;Likert, 1961;Seltzer & Bass, 1990;van Dierendonck, 2011), but unfortunately most of these theories proceeded in their early development "without sufficient attention to contextual contingencies" (Avolio, 2007, p. 27). This was in spite of the core premise that leadership behavior needed to vary in expression according to situational contingencies (Zaccaro et al., 1991). In sum, Zaccaro et al. (2018; see also Graeff, 1997;Yukl, 2011) characterized research on leadership situations (and leader-situation interactions) as: (a) being fragmented and lacking in coherence because it has incorporated a very wide range of leadership situational factors, (b) having confounded structural and psychological situations and more generally having underemphasized the latter, (c) having delineated nomological networks that exhibit shortcomings (e.g., viewing leadership situations and leader individual differences as orthogonal rather than as reciprocally related), and, perhaps as a result, (d) "suffer[ing] from conceptual weaknesses … and [having] not garnered strong evidentiary support" (p. 29).

These conclusions speak to the need for a more systematic taxonomy of underlying dimensions that can be used to classify the full range of situations that can influence leadership. Accordingly, the purpose of the present study was to develop and validate an empirically based taxonomy of leadership situations. Such a classification can offer several benefits for leadership research. First, This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

a taxonomy of leadership situations can help advance leadership theory by providing a basis for developing testable propositions about the situation in leadership research (Fleishman et al., 1984): for instance, defining linkages between leader attributes and behavioral patterns, and the core situational parameters that cue their engagement (Zaccaro et al., 2018). Second, a leadership situation taxonomy can facilitate comparisons across studies to derive generalizations around situational effects in leadership (Fleishman et al., 1984). Johns (2017) concluded that only 16% of leadership studies provided sufficient contextual information to afford such comparisons. This "context deficit" (Johns, 2018, p. 22) can be ameliorated by having a consensual classification system of situations that can be applied across different leadership contexts to facilitate situational comparisons. Such a system can offer core dimensions that apply across the range of leadership behavioral styles that have proliferated in the literature.

Third, a taxonomy of leadership situations can foster the generalization of past leadership research conducted in particular situations to new situations (Fleishman et al., 1984). For example, as new technologies and settings change the contexts of leadership, a classification of situational elements can be used to determine how observations and conclusions derived in more traditional leadership contexts (e.g., office or factory work) can be appliedor altered-in these more novel domains (e.g., remote work). This function of a taxonomy of leadership situations, along with the first two functions, establishes the basis for a fourth one: exposing knowledge gaps (Fleishman et al., 1984). We have noted that, despite the prominent conceptual placement of the situation in leadership research for over 60 years, there is still much uncertainty about how situations fundamentally influence leadership behaviors and outcomes either directly or through interaction with leader attributes. A classification of leadership situations can help organize prior leadership research and thereby highlight key holes in these domains and establish the basis for future research.

In subsequent sections, we discuss existing taxonomic efforts as they pertain to situations in general as well as leadership situations in particular, and we discuss why, these existing taxonomies notwithstanding, there remains a pressing need for a comprehensive, bottomup, empirically (and in particular lexically) derived taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics. To facilitate our discussion of those topics, however, it is first necessary to define a leadership situation and discuss the nomological network of leadership situations. In so doing, we will also elaborate on key distinctions between momentary and chronic situations and between structural and psychological leadership situations.

sections.header_norm: leadership situations definition and nomological network
paragraphs: As has been noted by several taxonomists (e.g., Fleishman et al., 1984;Rauthmann et al., 2014), definition should precede taxonomization (which in turn should precede measurement). In the socialpersonality psychology literature, a popular definition of a situation (Rauthmann & Sherman, 2019, p. 239) is: "a set of … circumstances that do not lie within a person, but in his or her surroundings … ." Situational concepts vary in their duration or stability, ranging from seconds or minutes to months or years (Rauthmann & Sherman, 2021). Just as "personality" can describe either the state of a specific person on a specific occasion (i.e., the personality state perspective) or else the regularities in the person's personality states across several occasions (i.e., the personality trait perspective; Fleeson & Jayawickreme, 2015), the situation can be either dynamic (i.e., the momentary situations perspective) or else relatively static (i.e., the chronic situations perspective).foot_1 Thus, the momentary leadership situations perspective might refer, for instance, to the situation encountered by the leader during a portion of a particular work shift. In contrast, the chronic leadership situations perspective might refer to the commonality across the momentary situations experienced by the leader in their leadership role or job in general.

When discussing existing research on leadership situations, we (see also Rauthmann et al., 2014) note the necessity of not simply referring to "the situation" but rather of being more precise in one's terminology. According to models of situation perception, the situation contains both objective and subjective components (Hattrup & Jackson, 1996;Jansen et al., 2013). An objective situation describes the structural or physical characteristics (i.e., "cues") of the situation and can describe who (is present other than the leader), what (is happening), when (it is occurring; e.g., time of day), where (the leadership is being enacted), and which (objects are present; e.g., mission statement; Parrigon et al., 2017). The subjective situation describes the psychological characteristics of the situation and results from a person's interpretation of the objective situation (e.g., as "important" or "unusually positive"), often in terms of perceived situational demands and affordances (Alaybek et al., 2018), making the subjective situation a function of not only the objective situation but also the leader's attributes or traits (Hattrup & Jackson, 1996).

Psychological situation characteristics and structural situation cues, therefore, map onto subjective and objective situations, respectively. Adapting existing general (i.e., non-leadership-specific) situation definitions (Johns, 2006, p. 386;Mowday & Sutton, 1993), we define structural leadership situation cues as work-related objects and actors (e.g., followers, associates, tasks, organizations) and psychological leadership situation characteristics as the leader's perceptions regarding the underlying psychological qualities of these structural factors.

In Figure 1, we present a conceptual model that incorporates both the psychological leadership situation characteristics that are the primary focus of this article and the structural leadership situation cues that are its secondary focus. In this model, structural situation cues are "filtered, evaluated, and attached with meaning" by the leader in the situation, using information processing techniques such as evaluation, interpretation, and construal (Path "a" in Figure 1; Rauthmann et al., 2014, p. 679), resulting in perceptions or construals of the psychological situation (Path "b" in Figure 1).

Perceptions of situations determine the behaviors a leader will enact in particular situations (Rauthmann et al., 2014). Compared to structural leadership situation cues, psychological leadership situation characteristics are more proximal to, and therefore, more likely to influence, leader behavior and subsequent outcomes (Paths "d" and "f" in Figure 1, respectively). For this reason, situation researchers suggest that "the most relevant level of situational analysis This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

for theory building and research is the [psychological] situation" (Hattrup & Jackson, 1996, p. 512; see also Mischel et al., 2002). Yet, in some leadership works (e.g., Oc, 2018;Porter & McLaughlin, 2006), the focus is more on the structural situation (e.g., where, who, when) rather than the psychological situation. In yet other leadership works, as noted also by Zaccaro et al. (2018), psychological situation characteristics (e.g., routineness, importance, clarity) are not differentiated cleanly from structural situation cues (e.g., task, follower, organization). For instance, instead of measuring "task complexity," leadership researchers should measure leaders' perceptions of situational "complexity" per se. This is because the psychological characteristic of "complexity" is more likely than the structural cue of "tasks" to influence leader behavior and outcomes-and because the psychological characteristic of "complexity" can be related to several structural cues other than merely "tasks."

Consequently, in the present study, we developed separate taxonomies of psychological leadership situation characteristics and structural leadership situation cues. Given the importance of psychological situation characteristics, we subsequently developed, assessed the robustness of, and validated a measure-the LSQbased on the taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics. We also examined the relationships of the psychological leadership situation characteristics with the structural leadership situation cues (which we assessed via topic modeling) to see which co-occurrences of people, environments, and tasks relate to or afford certain psychological leadership situation characteristics.

sections.header_raw: Prior Taxonomies of Situations in General
sections.header_norm: prior taxonomies of situations in general
paragraphs: General situation researchers-that is, researchers who study situations per se rather than those relevant to leadership in particular-have developed several empirical situational taxonomies (for a list, see Rauthmann & Sherman, 2021). Among the most wellaccepted of these are

Rauthmann et al.'s (2014) DIAMONDS (i.e., Duty, Intellect, Adversity, Mating, pOsitivity, Negativity, Deception, and Sociality) and Parrigon et al.'s (2017) CAPTION (i.e., Complexity, Adversity, Positive valence, Typicality, Importance, humOr, and Negative valence). These two taxonomies are well-developed and, importantly, do focus on psychological situation characteristics. Yet, there are several reasons why general situation taxonomies should not be applied in toto to leadership situations. First, leadership situations collectively represent what Rauthmann et al. (2014) refer to as a specific "class" (i.e., domain) of general situations. A class of situations encompasses a range of structural situation cues (along with an associated range of psychological situation characteristics) that is narrower than that observed for general situations. To provide just one example, structural leadership situations inherently involve two or more people, one of whom is the leader and the others are likely subordinates. paragraphs: Psychological Leadership Situation Characteristics (affordances and demands):

Structural Leadership Situation Cues (people, tasks, criteria, environments, settings): Letters "a" through "h" are labels for specific relationships to which we refer in various parts of this article. Information processing constructs and some individual difference constructs were not measured in the present article but are nonetheless included in the model for the sake of completeness (and to spur future research); each such construct is denoted by an asterisk (i.e., " * "). The precise temporal bounds of the correlates and outcomes will depend on whether the focus is on momentary or chronic situations. Momentary psychological leadership situation characteristics will be related most strongly to momentary structural leadership situation cues, personality states, momentary leader behaviors, and short-term outcomes. Chronic psychological leadership situation characteristics will be related most strongly to chronic structural leadership situation cues, personality traits, typical leader behaviors, and long-term outcomes.

paragraphs: contrast, some general situations may not involve any interpersonal component whatsoever, whereas others may involve two or more people of roughly equal standing in terms of status or power (e.g., siblings, classmates, or airplane passengers). Second, errors of commission and omission may occur when general situation taxonomies are applied to leadership situations specifically. For example, judging by the items Rauthmann et al. (2014, p. 686) used to measure their psychological situation characteristic of "Mating," this situation characteristic should be less relevant to leadership (vs. general) situations for a variety of reasons (e.g., lower likelihood and appropriateness of considering subordinates to be romantic partners; Segal, 2005). Similarly, judging by the items Parrigon et al. (2017, p. 664) used to measure their psychological situation characteristic of "Humor," this situation characteristic should be less relevant to leadership (vs. general) situations because norms of professional conduct in workplace and especially leadership situations suggest that leadership (vs. general) situations are less likely to be characterized as "goofy," "childish," or "wacky." Conversely, certain psychological situation characteristics may be more prevalent in leadership (vs. general) situations. For instance, psychological situation characteristics involving power differentials or some aspect of formal or informal reporting hierarchies (e.g., breadth or span of control vs. depth or chain of command; DeChurch et al., 2010) may be more likely in leadership (vs. general) situations. In sum, the psychological situation characteristics studied by social-personality psychologists should not simply be imported into and then imposed upon the leadership domain.

paragraphs: We note that there have been at least two prior top-down, conceptual (vs. empirical) efforts at a taxonomy that classifies aspects of the leader's situation (Hoffman & Lord, 2013;Oc, 2018). Oc (2018) applied Johns's (2006) general (i.e., non-leadership-specific) context taxonomy to the leadership domain, thereby offering a structural classification distinguishing between omnibus contextual elements (e.g., group demographic differences and economic conditions) and discrete contextual elements (e.g., task and physical elements). These largely reflect structural elements of the leader's situation (Johns, 2006). In contrast, Hoffman and Lord's (2013) constructed a taxonomy of event-level dimensions grounded conceptually in the perceiver's cognitive and affective processing (Mischel & Shoda, 1995) of features of events. In the present article, we adopt a perspective similar to Hoffman and Lord (2013) in that we agree that what is most important is how situations are perceived and processed; however, we relate-both conceptually (see Figure 1) and empirically-such information processing to structural leadership situation cues, the latter of which is Oc's (2018) focus. Thus, our framework integrates aspects of both the Oc (2018) and Hoffman and Lord (2013) classifications.

The present taxonomy contributes beyond the Hoffman and Lord (2013) and Oc (2018) taxonomies in several other ways. First, an interesting question that arises from the Hoffman and Lord (2013) taxonomy is whether events are fully analogous to situations or are instead a subset of situations. Specifically, Dinh and Lord (2012) noted that events are constrained by broader situational constructs (e.g., climate) that they collectively called context. This view is consistent with our aforementioned argument that situations can range from momentary to chronic. A taxonomy of leadership situations should therefore be flexible enough to incorporate both momentary and chronic situations.

Second, Hoffman and Lord (2013; see also Porter & McLaughlin, 2006) emphasized certain time-focused dimensions: one involving dynamic versus static situations and another involving past versus present versus future. However, we note that time should be viewed as potentially important for every situation dimension rather than being isolated into one or two "time-specific" dimensions. Sampling across time (e.g., past vs. present vs. future) is not the same as sampling across situations because situations at different times can often be quite similar in terms of their psychological ingredients (Dalal et al., 2015). Thus, the study of situations should not be confounded with the study of time; rather, each situational dimension should be viewed as having temporal aspects such as static and dynamic elements (or chronic and momentary ones).

Third, Hoffman and Lord (2013) generally emphasized bipolar dimensions such as (but by no means limited to) "positive versus negative." Extensive research on affect (e.g., Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996) has suggested that empirical findings often favor two unipolar dimensions (e.g., positive affect and negative affect) over a single bipolar dimension (e.g., hedonic tone). Similarly, both major taxonomies of general situations (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014) found support for unipolar positive and negative dimensions of general situations. This suggests that Hoffman and Lord's (2013) inherent focus on bipolar dimensions may not hold true empirically for the "positive versus negative" dimension and perhaps more broadly.

A final and broader issue-indeed, one that encapsulates most of the preceding issues-is that Oc's (2018) and Hoffman and Lord's (2013) taxonomies are conceptual and top-down. Both taxonomies represent thoughtful and important contributions to the leadership literature, but the concern is that conceptual taxonomies, as products of the theories on which they are based, may omit needed dimensions, include unneeded dimensions, or include dimensions that, collectively, are unstandardized in terms of construct breadth (Hambrick, 1984). As Ashton and Lee (2005, p. 9) noted in the context of taxonomies of personality:

when … variables are generated on the basis of … theories or on the basis of perceived practical utility, there is less reason to be confident that every dimension will be represented adequately and independently of the others.

The concern is exacerbated when it comes to measures derived from conceptual taxonomies. Measures of leadership situations from the Oc (2018) and the Hoffman and Lord (2013) conceptual leadership situation taxonomies do not yet exist. However, as Ashton and Lee (2005) argued in the context of personality taxonomies, the measures derived from conceptual taxonomiesunlike the lexical approach-not only do not provide a representative sampling of stimuli but also are quite different from and not easily comparable to each other. Indeed, as Parrigon et al. (2017, p. 661) suggested in the context of general situations, an empirically and in particular lexically derived taxonomy has the potential not only to capture the broadest possible list of situational descriptors but also, in so doing, to "integrate the findings from taxonomies derived using differing conceptual lenses." It therefore follows that the same would be true of leadership situations in particular. sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: To contribute to the literature on leadership situations beyond existing efforts (as described previously), we propose an empirically derived taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics using a well-accepted and commonly used approach: the lexical approach. The lexical approach has been the basis for empirical taxonomic efforts in the areas of personality (e.g., Ashton & Lee, 2005;John et al., 1991), values (e.g., De Raad et al., 2016), and general (i.e., non-leadership-specific) situations (e.g., Parrigon et al., 2017). The theory behind this approach is that all conceptualizations of both persons (e.g., personality, values) and situations that are sufficiently important will, sooner or later, be encoded in natural language-typically in the form of adjectives (John et al., 1988; see also Goldberg, 1992)-because people use language to describe themselves, one another, and the situations they encounter (Allport, 1937;Cattell, 1943;John et al., 1988;Saucier & Goldberg, 1996). Thus, the lexical approach is a "first principles" (or "blank slate") approach, starting typically from a list of all the adjectives available in dictionaries or other relevant linguistic sources. In the lexical approach to personality, participants use these adjectives (or adjective-driven sentences) to rate behavioral predispositions that are manifested regularly (i.e., personality traits) or in particular situations (i.e., personality states). In the case of psychological leadership situation characteristics in the present study, we asked leaders to use adjectives to rate either their leadership job in general (i.e., chronic situations) or specific recent leadership events (i.e., momentary situations). We used these adjective ratings to develop and validate a survey measure of the psychological leadership situation characteristics, which we have labeled the LSQ.

In addition to rating their situations via adjectives in the present study, leaders also described their specific recent leadership event (momentary situation condition) or their leadership job in general (chronic situation condition) via open-ended narratives, which we used to assess structural leadership situation types. Although previous general situation research (Rauthmann et al., 2014) empirically explored structural situation cues, their study of discrete structural situation cues-such as the location (e.g., conference room) or the other people (e.g., subordinates) or objects (e.g., strategic plan) present-represents a piecemeal approach. To capture common co-occurrences between various structural situation cues (e.g., meeting with subordinates in a conference room to discuss a strategic plan), the present study instead used topic modeling, a machine learning approach (Blei et al., 2003;Kosinski et al., 2016), to summarize typical structural leadership situation cue combinations from leaders' situation narratives.

Our conceptual model (Figure 1) provides an organizing framework (Rauthmann et al., 2014;Zaccaro et al., 2018) for the fragmented literature on leadership situations by delineating the nomological network of psychological leadership situation characteristics, including their relationship with structural leadership situation cues, leader behavior, leadership outcomes, and leader individual differences. This framework allows for the antecedents, correlates, and outcomes of psychological situation characteristics to be studied systematically.

Due to the complexity of the nomological network, a comprehensive test of Figure 1 is beyond the scope of the present article. Nonetheless, the present article does use the newly developed and validated measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics (the LSQ) to provide initial empirical tests of the nomological network. We could not formulate a priori hypotheses because we did not know in advance which dimensions (factors) would emerge for the psychological leadership situation characteristics, given that the lexical approach is inherently exploratory. In the absence of hypotheses, we interpreted the findings based on the extent to which they appeared consistent with those from relevant prior research.

First, we examined the relationships of the psychological leadership situation characteristics with the structural leadership situation cues (the product of Paths a and b in Figure 1). Second, focusing specifically on momentary (vs. chronic) leadership situations, we empirically examined the relationships of the psychological leadership situation characteristics with leader behavior in those situations (Path d in Figure 1; Dinh & Lord, 2012;Zaccaro et al., 2018) and with the outcomes of those situations (Path f in Figure 1; Kerr & Jermier, 1978;Podsakoff & MacKenzie, 1995). We assessed leadership behavior in momentary situations via leader initiating structure and consideration behaviors (Stogdill, 1963) and we assessed the outcomes of these momentary leadership situations via their valence (i.e., positive vs. negativity ;Vroom, 1964).foot_2

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: We used a multistep procedure to develop and validate the taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and the resulting measure. In so doing, we drew from best-practice recommendations from the literature on general (i.e., nondomainspecific) situation taxonomies (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014). We then developed a typology of structural leadership situation cues (via topic modeling) to which the focal taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics could be related. Finally, we validated the measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics by empirically testing portions of its nomological network via relationships with leader personality, leader behavior, outcomes of the situation, and the typology of structural leadership situation cues.

In so doing, we drew from best-practice recommendations from the literature on general (i.e., nondomainspecific) situation taxonomies (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014). We then developed a typology of structural leadership situation cues (via topic modeling) to which the focal taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics could be related. Finally, we validated the measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics by empirically testing portions of its nomological network via relationships with leader personality, leader behavior, outcomes of the situation, and the typology of structural leadership situation cues.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
sections.header_raw: Build a Corpus of Leadership-Relevant Text
sections.header_norm: build a corpus of leadership relevant text
paragraphs: Most existing taxonomy development efforts in the areas of both personality and the situation have used adjectives as the base unit of analysis (see, e.g., Edwards & Templeton, 2005;Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014). This is because: (a) adjectives are ubiquitous in virtually all languages, (b) descriptions of personality and situations are considerably more ubiquitous and unambiguous via adjectives than via other parts of speech (e.g., nouns, verbs), and (c) items in taxonomies that use sentences or phrases rather than single words are in practice composed primarily of adjectives along with the person or situation referent words (e.g., "I am" for personality; or "The situation is" for a situation; Saucier & Goldberg, 1996). Accordingly, we followed suit by using adjectives as the base unit of analysis (representative of psychological situation characteristics) for our taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics.

Previous taxonomic research obtained adjectives from various sources, such as a dictionary (in the case of the Big Five personality traits; John & Srivastava, 1999) or movie subtitles (in the case of a general taxonomy of situations; Parrigon et al., 2017). In the present study, as the goal was to develop a taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics, the population of adjectives came from four sources we believed would provide leadershiprelevant text. We describe these sources briefly here and in more detail in online Supplemental Material A.

The first source for leadership-relevant text was the leadership research literature: specifically, abstracts from context-and situationbased leadership research. Through Google Scholar title searches, using a combination of search terms to represent situations and leaders, we added 3,138 abstracts and accompanying keywords from leadership articles to the leadership-related corpus.

The second source was the 2010 Occupational Information Network (O * NET) taxonomy (National Center for O * NET Development, n.d.). We pulled occupational information, including job-oriented descriptors (e.g., work activities, work context, tasks) and workeroriented descriptors (e.g., work values) for 138 leadership occupations on O * NET (out of the 1,110 total occupations). The leadership occupations were identified by two industrial-organizational (I-O) psychologists, both of whom have leadership experience, have PhDs in I-O psychology, and work at an I-O consulting/research organization. Specifically, these subject matter experts (SMEs) flagged O * NET occupations that reflected "formal leadership" positions, meaning that an individual in that occupation would formally supervise at least one other person. Agreement in the flagging of leadership occupations was 97.03% (1,077 out of the 1,110 occupations).

The third source for leadership-relevant text was USAJOBS, an online job portal for federal agencies and organizations in the United States. Job postings can provide valuable information about the context of these positions. The postings describe the tasks and responsibilities for the job along with information about the organization itself. At two time points (August and November 2018), we gathered the job qualifications, duties, and responsibilities for 1,380 unique jobs flagged as supervisory by USAJOBS (out of 12,863 total jobs) via the USAJOBS application program interface and web scraping.

The final source was descriptions or narratives of situations encountered by leaders. These narratives were obtained in two ways. One was a data collection where we gathered situations from 51 leaders on https://Amazon.com's Mechanical Turk (MTurk), asking them to provide an open-ended description of a leadership situation they had experienced. The second involved gathering situations/scenarios from existing situational judgment tests, because situational judgment tests describe situations in their item stems. We gathered 685 leadership situations from situational judgment tests in leadership research journals articles as well as situational judgment tests developed by applied research/consulting firms for the selection, promotion, or assessment of leaders.

paragraphs: We used text cleaning techniques and parts of speech tagging used in natural language processing to identify and extract adjectives from the leadership-relevant corpus compiled from the four sources described above. The text was cleaned (e.g., replacing contractions, replacing numbers with the text equivalent, removing symbols) using the R package "tm" (Feinerer & Hornik, 2017) and parts of speech were tagged using the TreeTagger software (Schmid, 1994), which was run in R using the package koRpus (Michalke et al., 2017). Here, as elsewhere, manual spot checks were conducted to verify that the procedures worked as intended. After collapsing across sources, we extracted 6,429 unique adjectives from the leadership-relevant corpus. For information about the number of adjectives per corpus source, see Table 1.

paragraphs: Next, we reduced the number of 6,429 extracted adjectives so as to retain only the most leadership-relevant ones. Several of the sources used to build the leadership-relevant corpus came from the internet (e.g., USAJOBS postings, leadership abstracts); therefore, from these sources in particular, there was the likelihood of "nonwords" (e.g., acronyms or "words" from within hyperlinks) in the list of adjectives. If an adjective was not in a dictionary, it indicates that the adjective may not really be a word. 4 We used an aggregate of three dictionaries accessible in R (Ooms, 2018;Ooms & Hester, 2019;Rinker, 2013) and a dictionary created from a large text corpus (Pennington et al., 2014) to identify nonwords. An adjective was deemed a word and retained if at least three of the four dictionaries contained it. Using these dictionaries resulted in the removal of 1,631 adjectives.

Of the remaining 4,798 adjectives, we wanted to identify the ones most relevant to leadership situations. To accomplish this, we This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

used natural language processing techniques (specifically, versions of n-grams, which are neighboring or co-occurring sequences of words in a corpus, and word embeddings) to generate quantitative data associated with each adjective (Kumar & Paul, 2016;Pennington et al., 2014). These quantitative data reflected (a) the frequency with which adjectives appeared in the leadership-relevant corpus compared to their frequency in the English language generally (i.e., penalized unigrams), (b) the extent to which adjectives co-occurred with one or two situational and leadership keywords (i.e., bigrams and trigrams) in the leadership-relevant corpus, (c) the extent to which adjectives co-occurred with one or two situational and leadership keywords (i.e., bigrams and trigrams) in a corpus of the English language (the Corpus of Contemporary American English, which is the largest frequency-based word list of English available; Word Frequency Data, n.d.), and (d) the semantic similarity of the adjectives with situational and leadership keywords, as determined by word embeddings (see online Supplemental Material B, for more information about the generation of these keywords and criteria). These four criteria represented an adjective's relevance to leadership situations. If an adjective was missing two or more of the four quantitative data sources (penalized unigrams, bigrams/trigrams from the leadership-relevant corpus, bigrams/trigrams from a large English language corpus, word embeddings), it was removed from the adjective list (and from consideration as a situation characteristic) because it meant that the adjective was less relevant to leadership situations (as it was never used to describe them) and/or that the adjective was unlikely to be used to characterize leadership situations. This rule removed 2,183 adjectives. The resulting list contained 2,615 adjectives.

To summarize and aggregate these quantitative data (e.g., frequency, co-occurrence) for the 2,615 adjectives, we created a composite score where we created rank order variables for each of the four criteria (penalized unigrams, bigrams/trigrams from the leadership-relevant corpus, bigrams/trigrams from a large English language corpus, word embeddings) and then averaged these rankbased variables to create the criteria composite. There were several reasons for creating a rank-based criteria composite rather than a raw criteria composite. First, several of the criteria, particularly the bigrams and trigrams, were positively skewed given that many adjectives had few bigrams or trigrams. A rank-based composite accounted for variable skewness and improved the internal consistency of the composite. Second, using a rank-based criteria composite rather than a raw criteria composite improved the face validity of the top adjectives, where the rank-based criteria had more psychological characteristics within the top adjectives as determined by the first and second authors. The remaining 2,615 adjectives were rank ordered based on leadership relevance (i.e., the criteria composite score).

If an adjective was not in a dictionary, it indicates that the adjective may not really be a word. 4 We used an aggregate of three dictionaries accessible in R (Ooms, 2018;Ooms & Hester, 2019;Rinker, 2013) and a dictionary created from a large text corpus (Pennington et al., 2014) to identify nonwords.
sections.preview_tail: First, several of the criteria, particularly the bigrams and trigrams, were positively skewed given that many adjectives had few bigrams or trigrams. A rank-based composite accounted for variable skewness and improved the internal consistency of the composite. Second, using a rank-based criteria composite rather than a raw criteria composite improved the face validity of the top adjectives, where the rank-based criteria had more psychological characteristics within the top adjectives as determined by the first and second authors. The remaining 2,615 adjectives were rank ordered based on leadership relevance (i.e., the criteria composite score).

paragraphs: To this point in the process, identification of potentially leadershiprelevant adjectives was largely automated. However, we also felt it was critical to obtain SME input on the leadership-relevance of adjectives to further refine the working list. To reduce rater fatigue, SMEs only rated the most leadership-relevant adjectives on the list, as determined by the criteria composite described in the previous step. Specifically, SMEs rated 604 adjectives, including the 504 adjectives with criteria composite scores one standard deviation above the mean and the 100 adjectives with the next highest criteria composite scores (to provide a slightly better representation of the 2,615 adjectives). 5Four leader SMEs (including one academic and three practitioners) provided feedback on the 604 adjectives. These four SMEs all have PhDs in industrial and organizational psychology, are present leaders in their organization or academic institution and have a combined 75 years of experience in the practice of and/or research on leadership.

Leader SMEs were provided the definition of psychological leadership situation characteristics ("The leader's perceptions regarding the underlying qualities of these work-related objects and actors [e.g., follower, associates, organization, job demands, time]") and were asked to decide if each adjective describes or evaluates a leadership situation. Specifically, for each adjective, leader SMEs were asked whether the adjective fit within either (or both) of the following two sentences, "That leadership situation was ______" or "That was a ______ leadership situation." As the goal at this stage was to exclude adjectives not particularly relevant to leadership situations, an adjective was retained if it was rated as relevant to leadership situations (meaning it was rated as fitting within at least one of the sentences above) by at least three out of the four raters. Using this criterion, SMEs identified 237 out of the 604 adjectives as being able to describe or evaluate a leadership situation and these were therefore used in the next step. These 237 adjectives are listed in online Supplemental Material C. This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

sections.header_raw: Leader Situation Narratives and Situation Ratings
sections.header_norm: leader situation narratives and situation ratings
paragraphs: To determine the structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics, leaders described and rated situations they encountered using the 237 adjectives derived above. In the attempt to create a taxonomy that generalizes across leader hierarchical levels and occupation types, we recruited leaders using two online sampling pools (i.e., panels): Qualtrics and MTurk. To ensure the recruitment of leaders and to avoid the gray area of possible informal leadership roles (where it is nearly impossible to know for sure if the person has subordinates in the formal sense), we recruited leaders in formal leadership positions only, including only those who formally supervise one or more subordinates.

paragraphs: Qualtrics. Survey responses were collected via the Qualtrics Panels Team. Qualtrics actively recruited leaders from their business pools and prescreened them before they took the survey (e.g., ensured they were leaders and were working full time). Participants who missed attention checks or did not meet eligibility criteria (e.g., presently manages at least one employee) were directed to the end of the survey and not included in the responses received from Qualtrics. Of the 790 responses received from Qualtrics, we removed 34 due to inattentive response patterns in the adjective ratings, 13 due to job titles (e.g., we did not consider teachers to be conventional leaders), and 227 due to low-quality openended narratives. We retained 516 leader responses from Qualtrics. This sample was composed of 58.91% women, with an average age of 43.70 years (SD = 13.30 years). On average, these leaders worked 44.00 hours per week (SD = 6.87 hr) and came from all three hierarchical levels of leadership (due to intentional stratified sampling)-32.75% frontline leaders (representative job titles: manager, supervisor, office manager, and shift supervisor), 34.69% mid-level leaders (representative job titles: information technology director, senior manager, regional manager, and director of operations), and 32.56% senior-level leaders (representative job titles: president, chief information officer, owner, and partner)-and a wide range of job families. See online Supplemental Material D for more details about data cleaning and the sample.

MTurk. To ensure that MTurk participants were indeed leaders, we included a prescreen questionnaire whose purpose was not communicated to participants. Overall,6,192 MTurk participants completed the prescreen questionnaire. Of them, 1,178 (19.02%) passed the prescreen (i.e., identified as a leader by stating they were frontline, mid-level, or senior-level leadership and that they managed at least one employee, worked 32 or more hours per week, and scored at least 50% on the Wordsum vocabulary test to demonstrate English proficiency; Malhotra & Krosnick, 2007). Of these 1,178 leaders, 808 completed the second, focal survey (for a 68.59% response rate) and were paid $2.50 for their responses. Similar to the Qualtrics sample, we used a plethora of data cleaning methods, including eligibility criteria (e.g., presently manages at least one employee), response patterns (e.g., straight line responses, percent of missing data), attention checks, and open-ended narrative quality (e.g., low effort responses, indication that they did not understand the prompt). After data cleaning, the final sample of 643 MTurk leaders was composed of 44.63% women. The average age of the sample was 38.60 years (SD = 10.40 years). On average, these leaders worked 43.30 hours per week (SD = 6.20 hr) and came from all three hierarchical levels of leadership-46.50% frontline leaders (representative job titles: manager, supervisor, retail store manager, and property manager), 38.26% mid-level leaders (representative job titles: division director, operations manager, logistics officer, and Vice President control manager), and 15.24% seniorlevel leaders (representative job titles: owner, chief executive officer, chief information officer, and superintendent of schools)-and reflected a variety of job families. See online Supplemental Material D for more details about the sample.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: We evaluated whether the structure of psychological situation characteristics varies across momentary versus chronic situations by randomly assigning leaders to describe either a situation encountered on a recent workday or their typical leadership-related work situation. For momentary situations (50% of the overall sample), participants were asked to describe a leadership situation encountered on the previous workday at a randomly assigned quarter of the workday (e.g., if they worked 8 hours, the first 2 hours would be Quarter 1). To prevent under-sampling of nonroutine situations, we collected data from another subsample (10% of the momentary sample; i.e., 5% of the overall sample), using evidence-based strategies to maximize accurate recall (e.g., Christianson & Loftus, 1991), in which leaders described the most recent unusual leadership-related situation they encountered in the previous month. For typical (i.e., chronic) leadership-related work situations (the remaining 50% of the sample), participants were asked to describe their leadership job in general.

Each leader first provided a narrative description of their situation, their behavior, and the results (outcomes). Next, leaders provided ratings on the psychological leadership situation characteristics (i.e., the list of 237 adjectives from the previous step). Leaders rated the extent to which each adjective described the leadership situation in question-that is, either the momentary situation or the leadership job in general (i.e., chronic situation)using a 5-point Likert-type scale (1 = not at all characteristic of the [situation or job], 5 = very characteristic of the [situation or job]), which is consistent with best-practice recommendations for scale development and validation (Hinkin, 1998). Finally, leaders completed the Big Five Inventory-10 to measure Conscientiousness, Agreeableness, Extraversion, Neuroticism, and Openness with two questions per trait (Rammstedt & John, 2007).

sections.header_raw: Analyze the Psychological Characteristics of Leadership Situations
sections.header_norm: analyze the psychological characteristics of leadership situations
paragraphs: To uncover the dimensionality of psychological leadership situation characteristics, we divided the overall online panel sample (from Qualtrics and MTurk) of 1,159 leaders into a calibration sample (approximately 65% of responses; n = 752) and a validation sample (approximately 35% of responses; n = 407). A stratified random sampling technique was used, such that leaders were randomly assigned to the calibration and validation samples to ensure an even distribution of cases based on subsample (Qualtrics vs. MTurk), condition (momentary vs. chronic), and leader characteristics (leader hierarchical level, leader gender).

sections.header_raw: Exploratory Factor Analysis of Adjective Ratings
sections.header_norm: exploratory factor analysis of adjective ratings
paragraphs: For the calibration sample, we performed an exploratory factor analysis (EFA) with maximum likelihood extraction and oblimin rotation (an oblique rotation method) using the "fa" function in the "psych" R package (Revelle, 2018). For the initial model, 6 we used parallel analysis and Velicier's minimum average partial test approaches to determine the number of factors to extract (O'Connor, 2000). These tests suggested 13 and 23 factors, respectively. Opting for parsimony in the solution, we ran and examined the 13-factor solution and found that seven factors did not have at least three strongly loading items (with loadings greater than |.50|), suggesting that these factors were weak and unstable (Costello & Osborne, 2005). Thus, we ran an EFA model with six factors on the calibration sample and found that the factors were stable with at least three strongly loading items per factor. With the number of factors determined, we followed best practices (e.g., Worthington & Whittaker, 2006) and removed the adjectives in the six-factor model with low factor loadings (lower than |.32|) and/or high crossloadings (loadings of |.32| or higher on two or more factors) and re-ran the six-factor model on the calibration sample, flagging and removing any adjectives that now had weak factor loadings and high cross-loadings. Online Supplemental Material E provides more information about the EFA model iterations. A final six-factor model with 172 adjectives demonstrated strong loadings and low cross-loadings. To summarize the EFA model, Table 2 provides the top 10 loading adjectives per factor (i.e., 60 adjectives in total). Online Supplemental Material E (specifically, Supplemental Table E2) contains the full 172 adjective solution.

Findings from the EFA suggest that it should be possible to develop a taxonomy for psychological leadership situation characteristics using the six dimensions (factors) that emerged. Based on their content (specifically, based on the top 10 words per factor), we named the six dimensions Positive Uniqueness, Importance, Negativity, Scope, Typicality, and Ease. 7 The following section (see also Table 3) provides definitions for the dimensions as well as related situation characteristics from the leadership literature and related dimensions from general (i.e., nondomain-specific) situation taxonomies.

sections.header_raw: Dimensions of Psychological Leadership Situation Characteristics
sections.header_norm: dimensions of psychological leadership situation characteristics
paragraphs: To name and define the dimensions of psychological leadership situation characteristics, we followed several best practices recommended by Podsakoff et al. (2016). First, our understanding of the nature of each dimension was informed by the top 10 loading adjectives for each factor, and to an even greater extent the top 4-5 adjectives, so that the attributes included in the definition of a dimension reflected the dimension's empirically derived operationalization and focused on its most representative characteristics. Second, we clearly defined the opposite pole of each dimension. Third, we searched online dictionaries and thesauri to ensure that we understood the meanings we were assigning to dimensions via the names (labels) we chose for them. Fourth, in our definitions, we identified the commonalities between our empirical leadership taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and other relevant literature: specifically, Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual leadership situation taxonomy and two recent but influential general situation taxonomies (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014). Where possible, we chose dimension names that reflected those in these previous taxonomies, so as to reduce construct proliferation. Finally, two SMEs (one coauthor of this article who is a leadership researcher and another coauthor who is a situation researcher) reviewed the dimension names and definitions.

Positive Uniqueness. The dimension of Positive Uniqueness captures the extent to which the situation is unique or special by being exciting, interesting, or featuring creativity. Situations high in Positive Uniqueness are perceived as pleasant and potentially related to personal satisfaction or impressive outcomes associated with the situation. Our empirical study reveals an overlap between two of Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual dimensions (extraordinary vs. ordinary, positive vs. negative), where high scores on Positive Uniqueness 8 capture extraordinary situations that are positive in nature. The Positive Uniqueness dimension does not capture negative extraordinary events, such as threats. Similar to research on positive and negative affect, which suggests two unipolar, relatively independent scales (e.g., low positive affect does not equate to high negative affect; Watson et al., 1988), low scores on the Positive Uniqueness dimension do not reflect negativity but rather neutral, unexciting, and standard situations absent of unique positive features. Furthermore, the Intellect dimension described in a previous general situation taxonomy (Rauthmann et al., 2014), where a situation affords an opportunity to demonstrate intellectual capacity or express unusual ideas or points of view, is similar to characteristics such as "creative" and "unique" in the present Positive Uniqueness dimension.

Importance. The dimension of Importance captures the importance and necessity of the situation, possibly related to its recency or immediacy, or because the situation is highly essential, specific, and relevant. The significance and importance of this dimension shares similarities with the situational components described in Vroom and Yetton's (1973) normative decision-making model, where the importance of the quality of the decision can influence the leader's behavior. We named this dimension after the general (non-leadership-specific) situation taxonomy dimension of Importance, which captures how well the situation might lead to the attainment of goals via functionality or goal-oriented actions (Parrigon et al., 2017). This dimension of Importance, specifically This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. 6 The model covariance matrix was initially nonpositive definite, which is possible if there is a linear dependency among the variables. To identify such dependencies, we identified adjective synonyms using the wordnet dictionary and package in R (Feinerer et al., 2016) and examined the correlations between these adjectives. Two pairs of adjectives (historic and historical; global and worldwide) demonstrated high correlations (r = .72-.77). We addressed the nonpositive definite correlation matrix by retaining the adjective within each pair (viz., historic, global) that had a larger mean and standard deviation (i.e., the adjective that was more characteristic of situations on average but also more variable across situations) and removing the other adjective from each pair (viz., historical, worldwide). We conducted a series of EFA model iterations using 235 adjectives. 7 To aid recall of the dimension names, we offer the mnemonic "SPINET": s(cope), p(ositive uniqueness), i(mportance), n(egativity), e(ase), and t(ypicality).

8 An alternative name for this dimension, which would avoid construct proliferation and reflect the overlap of this empirical taxonomy dimension with two anchors (positive, extraordinary) from Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy, would be "Positive Extraordinariness." However, such a name is extraordinarily (pun intended) cumbersome. Our chosen dimension name-Positive Uniqueness-at least maintains the "positive" nature of Hoffman and Lord's dimension, a feature also seen across many general situation taxonomies (e.g., see the Positivity and Positive Valence dimensions in Rauthmann et al., 2014 andParrigon et al., 2017, respectively). the essential or required aspects, also shares similarities with the general situation taxonomy dimension of Duty, wherein a job needs to be done and people are being counted on to get the job done (Rauthmann et al., 2014). The leader may perceive the situation as more essential and important if they perceive the situation as more personally relevant than irrelevant to goals and interests-an aspect of this empirical dimension that captures the personally relevant versus irrelevant event dimension in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy. Situations low on the Importance dimension include those that are viewed as unnecessary, inessential, and unimportant to the leader, potentially because they are irrelevant to the leader's goals and interests.

Negativity. The Negativity dimension captures the extent to which the situation is negative, characterized by hostility, inappropriateness, or ineffectiveness. This Negativity dimension captures an interpersonal aspect of situations, albeit interactions or relationships that have a negative valence. Such relationships and cohesion (or lack thereof) have been reflected in a variety of leadership situation theories via leader-member relationships (Fiedler & Garcia, 1987) and group (dis)agreement, harmony, cohesion, and support (Bass et al., 1975;Hunt & Osborn, 1982;Kerr & Jermier, 1978;Vroom & Jago, 1988). The negative portion of the positive versus negative event valence dimension in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy falls within this Negativity dimension. Where the aforementioned Positive Uniqueness dimension captures the positive valence aspect of Hoffman and Lord (2013) taxonomy, this Negativity dimension captures the negative valence, such that higher ratings on this dimension reflect perceptions of hostility, dysfunction, and ineffectiveness. Similar to the positive and negative affect literature, lower scores on this Negativity dimension do not reflect positivity but instead merely the absence of negativity.
Finding such a factor in the present work is consistent with previous general situation taxonomies, such as

Parrigon et al.'s (2017) Negative Valence dimension that includes malicious situations. However, the present Negativity dimension includes terms that are more functional in nature (e.g., poor, abusive, dysfunctional) compared to Parrigon et al.'s Negative Valence dimension which includes terms that are more affective and emotional in nature (e.g., despicable, repulsive, grotesque). The present Negativity dimension is closer to Rauthmann et al.'s (2014) general situation dimensions of Adversity (being criticized, blamed) and Deception (being hostile, undermined) dimensions compared to Rauthmann et al.'s own Negativity dimension (anxiety, frustration).

Scope. The dimension of Scope captures the breadth of the situation (e.g., international, global, federal). The perceptions of breadth can depend upon the type of organization (e.g., federal, foreign), the structure of the organization (e.g., delegation or departmentation), the diversity and size of the team or organization (e.g., national or multinational), and the physical distance between the leader and subordinates (as some teams can be local and centralized, yet others are national, worldwide, or virtual; Hunt & Osborn, 1982;Kerr & Jermier, 1978;Melcher, 1979).

Scope also covers the broader, external situation (e.g., the organization's environment). For instance, leadership situations can include a diversity of federal, economic, and cultural factors (Bass et al., 1975;Hunt & 1982). The broader, external situation can influence the work of leaders at all hierarchical levels. An environmental or societal crisis or a global trend in demand for particular products or services, not to mention a global pandemic like COVID-19, could require upper level leaders to change course in market outreach and investments and could require frontline leaders to accommodate changes in production schedules and staff availability. Thus, perceptions of Scope can reflect breadth and diversity within the organization (e.g., companies with international influence), as well as the outside environment (e.g., global or nationwide factors). Situations perceived as low in Scope may be narrow and insulated, ignoring the breadth or diversity of functions or teams nor the outside environment. No dimensions in the general situational literature and their related taxonomies reflect these situation characteristics (Rauthmann & Sherman, 2018). This suggests that the dimension of Scope is particularly important to leadership (vs., say, family or leisure) situations, perhaps due to the interconnected, just-in-time, and often global nature that provides the context for the functioning of upper level and, increasingly, even frontline leaders.

Typicality. The dimension of Typicality covers the ongoing, constant, and familiar nature of the situation. The commonness of a situation has been recognized in the general situation literature via a Typicality dimension in such taxonomies as well (e.g., Parrigon et al., 2017), and thus, when naming this dimension, we followed suit. This dimension reflects the extent to which the situation is stable, routine, and methodologically invariant (potentially because the situation occurs daily; Kerr & Jermier, 1978). Situations low in Typicality are deviations from the norm, uncommon, or infrequent. This dimension conceptually covers situation characteristics in leadership situation theories like task routineness and monotony (Bass et al., 1975;Kerr & Jermier, 1978) or task predictability (House, 1996) and reflects the familiar and ordinary portion of the familiar versus novel and extraordinary versus ordinary dimensions, respectively, in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy.

Ease. The dimension of Ease captures the extent to which a situation is simple, easy, and conventional, perhaps related to a lack of complexity and intensity. The leadership literature has tended to discuss situation characteristics at the other end of the Ease spectrum, as reflected by adjectives that loaded negatively onto the Ease factor, such as "complex," "complicated," and "challenging" (e.g., task complexity; Bass et al., 1975;Melcher, 1979). Similarly, the low end of the Ease dimension has been captured in the general situational literature as Complexity (emotionally or conceptually complex situations; Parrigon et al., 2017).

The Ease dimension conceptually captures aspects of Hoffman and Lord's (2013) dimension of familiar versus novel events. Familiar events meet expectations and do not require interpretation or learning, and thus are likely to be perceived as easy and basic. Alternatively, a novel or disruptive situation requires learning and could be perceived as challenging and complex.

However, Hoffman and Lord's (2013) familiar versus novel dimension also shares conceptual similarities with the present Typicality dimension, where familiar events are likely quotidian, ongoing, and/or constant, and with the present Positive Uniqueness dimension, because the novel aspects of events could be perceived as special and unique. Thus, when compared to the present empirical taxonomy, Hoffman and Lord's conceptual dimension of familiar versus novel events may actually be multidimensional.

Summary. The results of our analyses indicate that these six factors cover the dimensions of psychological characteristics in leadership situations. Our descriptions of these dimensions identified similarities with situation-based leadership theories and This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

taxonomies and general situation taxonomies as well as omissions in the general taxonomies when applied to leadership situations (viz., Scope). To avoid construct proliferation, we named two of the present psychological situation dimensions Typicality and Importance after their counterparts in Parrigon et al.'s (2017) general situation taxonomy.

There are also extraneous dimensions in the general situation taxonomies when applied to leadership situations, such that several general situation dimensions were not reflected in these six dimensions of psychological leadership situations. Specifically, the general taxonomies presented dimensions of Humor (e.g., wacky, mischievous, goofy, childish; Parrigon et al., 2017), Mating (stimuli that could be construed sexually; Rauthmann et al., 2014), and Sociality (where social interaction is possible; Rauthmann et al., 2014). The dimensions of Humor and Mating were likely omitted from this leadership situation taxonomy due to the lower relevance or prevalence of these characteristics in leadership situations. Conversely, the dimension of Sociality was likely omitted from this leadership situation taxonomy because of its overwhelming prevalence across all leadership situations: Leadership situations are inherently social (i.e., for someone to be a leader, there must be someone else to be led).

Our use of the lexical approach, which is a "first principles" (or "blank slate") approach, also revealed dimensional differences between our empirical taxonomy and those of preceding conceptual taxonomies of leadership situations. We did not find support for the inclusion of three of Hoffman and Lord's (2013) dimensions (microvs. macro-level, static vs. dynamic, and past vs. present vs. future). In our view, these characteristics are potentially applicable to all psychological situation dimensions and are therefore instead appropriately tested via moderator analyses involving momentary versus chronic situations. Hoffman and Lord's four remaining dimensions (familiar vs. novel, extraordinary vs. ordinary, positive vs. negative, and relevant vs. irrelevant) overlapped with four of our dimensions (Positive Uniqueness, Negativity, Typicality, and Ease), but mismatches in dimension poles and conceptual coverage were nonetheless apparent. For instance, the present taxonomy empirically found different dimensions for positivity and negativity (in line with general empirical situation taxonomies and the affect literature) whereas Hoffman and Lord had one bipolar positive versus negative dimension. Furthermore, our empirical dimension of Positive Uniqueness reflected not only the positive pole of Hoffman and Lord's positive versus negative valence dimension, but also the extraordinary pole of their extraordinary versus ordinary dimension, as well as the novel (albeit, the positive novel) pole of their familiar versus novel dimension.
