the essential or required aspects, also shares similarities with the general situation taxonomy dimension of Duty, wherein a job needs to be done and people are being counted on to get the job done (Rauthmann et al., 2014). The leader may perceive the situation as more essential and important if they perceive the situation as more personally relevant than irrelevant to goals and interests-an aspect of this empirical dimension that captures the personally relevant versus irrelevant event dimension in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy. Situations low on the Importance dimension include those that are viewed as unnecessary, inessential, and unimportant to the leader, potentially because they are irrelevant to the leader's goals and interests.

Negativity. The Negativity dimension captures the extent to which the situation is negative, characterized by hostility, inappropriateness, or ineffectiveness. This Negativity dimension captures an interpersonal aspect of situations, albeit interactions or relationships that have a negative valence. Such relationships and cohesion (or lack thereof) have been reflected in a variety of leadership situation theories via leader-member relationships (Fiedler & Garcia, 1987) and group (dis)agreement, harmony, cohesion, and support (Bass et al., 1975;Hunt & Osborn, 1982;Kerr & Jermier, 1978;Vroom & Jago, 1988). The negative portion of the positive versus negative event valence dimension in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy falls within this Negativity dimension. Where the aforementioned Positive Uniqueness dimension captures the positive valence aspect of Hoffman and Lord (2013) taxonomy, this Negativity dimension captures the negative valence, such that higher ratings on this dimension reflect perceptions of hostility, dysfunction, and ineffectiveness. Similar to the positive and negative affect literature, lower scores on this Negativity dimension do not reflect positivity but instead merely the absence of negativity.

Finding such a factor in the present work is consistent with previous general situation taxonomies, such as

Parrigon et al.'s (2017) Negative Valence dimension that includes malicious situations. However, the present Negativity dimension includes terms that are more functional in nature (e.g., poor, abusive, dysfunctional) compared to Parrigon et al.'s Negative Valence dimension which includes terms that are more affective and emotional in nature (e.g., despicable, repulsive, grotesque). The present Negativity dimension is closer to Rauthmann et al.'s (2014) general situation dimensions of Adversity (being criticized, blamed) and Deception (being hostile, undermined) dimensions compared to Rauthmann et al.'s own Negativity dimension (anxiety, frustration).

Scope. The dimension of Scope captures the breadth of the situation (e.g., international, global, federal). The perceptions of breadth can depend upon the type of organization (e.g., federal, foreign), the structure of the organization (e.g., delegation or departmentation), the diversity and size of the team or organization (e.g., national or multinational), and the physical distance between the leader and subordinates (as some teams can be local and centralized, yet others are national, worldwide, or virtual; Hunt & Osborn, 1982;Kerr & Jermier, 1978;Melcher, 1979).

Scope also covers the broader, external situation (e.g., the organization's environment). For instance, leadership situations can include a diversity of federal, economic, and cultural factors (Bass et al., 1975;Hunt & 1982). The broader, external situation can influence the work of leaders at all hierarchical levels. An environmental or societal crisis or a global trend in demand for particular products or services, not to mention a global pandemic like COVID-19, could require upper level leaders to change course in market outreach and investments and could require frontline leaders to accommodate changes in production schedules and staff availability. Thus, perceptions of Scope can reflect breadth and diversity within the organization (e.g., companies with international influence), as well as the outside environment (e.g., global or nationwide factors). Situations perceived as low in Scope may be narrow and insulated, ignoring the breadth or diversity of functions or teams nor the outside environment. No dimensions in the general situational literature and their related taxonomies reflect these situation characteristics (Rauthmann & Sherman, 2018). This suggests that the dimension of Scope is particularly important to leadership (vs., say, family or leisure) situations, perhaps due to the interconnected, just-in-time, and often global nature that provides the context for the functioning of upper level and, increasingly, even frontline leaders.

Typicality. The dimension of Typicality covers the ongoing, constant, and familiar nature of the situation. The commonness of a situation has been recognized in the general situation literature via a Typicality dimension in such taxonomies as well (e.g., Parrigon et al., 2017), and thus, when naming this dimension, we followed suit. This dimension reflects the extent to which the situation is stable, routine, and methodologically invariant (potentially because the situation occurs daily; Kerr & Jermier, 1978). Situations low in Typicality are deviations from the norm, uncommon, or infrequent. This dimension conceptually covers situation characteristics in leadership situation theories like task routineness and monotony (Bass et al., 1975;Kerr & Jermier, 1978) or task predictability (House, 1996) and reflects the familiar and ordinary portion of the familiar versus novel and extraordinary versus ordinary dimensions, respectively, in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy.

Ease. The dimension of Ease captures the extent to which a situation is simple, easy, and conventional, perhaps related to a lack of complexity and intensity. The leadership literature has tended to discuss situation characteristics at the other end of the Ease spectrum, as reflected by adjectives that loaded negatively onto the Ease factor, such as "complex," "complicated," and "challenging" (e.g., task complexity; Bass et al., 1975;Melcher, 1979). Similarly, the low end of the Ease dimension has been captured in the general situational literature as Complexity (emotionally or conceptually complex situations; Parrigon et al., 2017).

The Ease dimension conceptually captures aspects of Hoffman and Lord's (2013) dimension of familiar versus novel events. Familiar events meet expectations and do not require interpretation or learning, and thus are likely to be perceived as easy and basic. Alternatively, a novel or disruptive situation requires learning and could be perceived as challenging and complex.

However, Hoffman and Lord's (2013) familiar versus novel dimension also shares conceptual similarities with the present Typicality dimension, where familiar events are likely quotidian, ongoing, and/or constant, and with the present Positive Uniqueness dimension, because the novel aspects of events could be perceived as special and unique. Thus, when compared to the present empirical taxonomy, Hoffman and Lord's conceptual dimension of familiar versus novel events may actually be multidimensional.

paragraphs: To corroborate the factor structure suggested by the EFA models, we conducted a confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) on the validation sample (n = 407) using the "cfa" function in the "lavaan" R package (Rosseel, 2012). Prior to conducting the CFA, the 172 adjectives in the final EFA model were further reduced in an effort to create a concise scale, using two criteria (e.g., Stanton et al., 2002). The main criterion for adjective retention was the size of factor loadings (to capture prototypical adjectives for each factor, and to maximize internal consistency), such that the four items with the top loadings on each focal factor (from the EFA) were selected for each CFA factor. However, to reduce the risk of creating a "bloated specific" (Cattell, 1978) measure consisting of highly conceptually redundant items associated with a particular factor, the fifth highest loading adjective was added to the shortened measure of a factor if (but only if) it contributed a unique conceptual component to the construct (i.e., if the fifth adjective was not a synonym for any of the top four loading adjectives). Our approach was consistent with Hinkin's (1998) best-practice recommendations for scale development and validation, which note the limited benefits of using more than five items to measure each construct. These scale reduction techniques resulted in the LSQ, a six-factor measure of 27 adjectives representing the psychological characteristics of leadership situations. Table 3 contains the items for the LSQ.

The CFA on the validation sample (n = 407) supported the sixdimensional structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics reflected in the LSQ. The six-factor structure fit the data well (χ 2 = 715.21, df = 309, comparative fit index [CFI] = .92, Tucker-Lewis index [TLI] = .91, root-mean-square error of approximation [RMSEA] = .06, and standardized root-mean-square residual [SRMR] = .07). Descriptive statistics, scale reliabilities (Cronbach's α), and correlations can be found in Table 4.

We conducted measurement invariance analyses to empirically examine the structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics across situation condition (momentary vs. chronic), leader hierarchical level (frontline vs. upper levelfoot_7 ), and leader genderfoot_8 (male leaders vs. female leaders). In short, measurement invariance tests found that the structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics did not vary appreciably across momentary versus chronic situations, leader hierarchical level, or leader gender, providing generalizability evidence for the LSQ. Mean level differences across the moderators provide important insights into relevant leadership theories. Online Supplemental Material E provides our results and interpretations of these measurement invariance analyses.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: We noted earlier that leadership situations contain not only psychological (i.e., subjective) characteristics but also structural (i.e., more objective) cues. Up to this point, our analyses focused on psychological leadership situation characteristics, as the psychological situation (or the way the leader perceives the situation) will exert a greater influence on leader behavior. However, structural leadership situation cues do have a role in shaping psychological leadership situation characteristics, and understanding the range of structural situation cues is therefore helpful in understanding psychological situation characteristics. The analyses described in this section are intended to extract structural leadership situation cues.

In an open-ended question in the survey described previously, each leader was asked to describe a momentary or chronic leadership situation, including the people, objects, and tasks involved. Such narratives provide a vivid illustration of the structural situation cues (who, what, where) within leadership situations. Unlike the psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy, for which This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

we also developed a survey measure (the LSQ), we developed a typology of structural leadership situation cues by identifying themes in these narratives (via topic modeling), and therefore, we do not provide an accompanying self-report survey measure. Specifically, to identify themes in these narratives, we used the "Structural Topic Model" (stm) package in R to run topic models (Roberts et al., 2019) on the leaders' responses. First, we cleaned the leader narratives using the text cleaning methods described in the "Extract Leadership-Relevant Adjectives" step. Next, we ran diagnostics on models of varying different topic numbers to determine the best fitting number of topics (Chang et al., 2009;Kosinski et al., 2016). Examining the heldout likelihood (the predictive strength of each model on a heldout data set) along with semantic coherence (the average interpretability of topics) suggested that the best fitting models contained five to seven topics. We used the visualization tool LDAvis (Sievert & Shirley, 2015), adapted to view structural topic model results (Roberts et al., 2019), to visualize the topics in terms of their overlap, coverage of the narrative space, and top words. Conceptually, a model of six topics best fit the situational narratives. To help define these six topics (i.e., combinations of structural leadership situation cues), we examined the top words and narratives (based on topic probabilities that represent the prevalence of topics within the narratives and words within the topics) for each topic, which can be found in online Supplemental Material F. Table 5 defines these six topics or combinations of structural situation cues: Market/Business Needs, Barriers to Effectiveness, Interpersonal Resources, Deviations/Changes, Team Objectives, and Logistics. Each topic/structural leadership situation cue combination captures individual structural situation cues such as who is typically in the situation, what (e.g., task) is typically happening in the situation, and where (e.g., industry) are these situations typically taking place. For instance, the structural situation cue combination Team Objectives reflect the people (e.g., subordinates) and tasks related to a leader establishing team member roles, delegating tasks, and building or maintaining collaboration. Meanwhile, Deviations/Changes highlight work environments, like restaurants and retail stores, where crowded restaurants or shops could result in tight timeframes and overwhelmed staff. Unlike Deviation/Changes that are characterized by more people present, Barriers to Effectiveness tend to include fewer people, including only those people (e.g., subordinate, client) directly involved in the confrontation or resolution.

Although the structural leadership situation cue combinations reflect "types" of leadership situations, a leader's situation can reflect every type simultaneously, albeit to varying extents. Structural topic modeling provides the proportion for which each leader's situation narrative is reflective of each structural leadership situation cue combination. Thus, every situation narrative has six structural scores (i.e., six proportions), each of which reflects one of the six structural leadership situation cue combinations. A situational narrative that is primarily reflective of one particular cue combination has a high structural score for that cue combination, but it nonetheless also has structural scores for the other five cue combinations (although these other scores are much lower).

Using structural topic modeling, we also examined the difference in topic proportions (e.g., how much a situation is associated with a topic) across groups, which is parallel to tests for scale score differences (e.g., how much a situation is characterized by a dimension) between groups. Specifically, we looked at differences in topic proportions for (a) hierarchical level of leader (frontline vs. mid-level vs. senior-level), (b) situation condition (momentary vs. chronic), and (c) leader gender (men vs. women). Online Supplemental Material F contains a summary of our results.

In an open-ended question in the survey described previously, each leader was asked to describe a momentary or chronic leadership situation, including the people, objects, and tasks involved. Such narratives provide a vivid illustration of the structural situation cues (who, what, where) within leadership situations. Unlike the psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy, for which This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

we also developed a survey measure (the LSQ), we developed a typology of structural leadership situation cues by identifying themes in these narratives (via topic modeling), and therefore, we do not provide an accompanying self-report survey measure. Specifically, to identify themes in these narratives, we used the "Structural Topic Model" (stm) package in R to run topic models (Roberts et al., 2019) on the leaders' responses. First, we cleaned the leader narratives using the text cleaning methods described in the "Extract Leadership-Relevant Adjectives" step. Next, we ran diagnostics on models of varying different topic numbers to determine the best fitting number of topics (Chang et al., 2009;Kosinski et al., 2016). Examining the heldout likelihood (the predictive strength of each model on a heldout data set) along with semantic coherence (the average interpretability of topics) suggested that the best fitting models contained five to seven topics. We used the visualization tool LDAvis (Sievert & Shirley, 2015), adapted to view structural topic model results (Roberts et al., 2019), to visualize the topics in terms of their overlap, coverage of the narrative space, and top words. Conceptually, a model of six topics best fit the situational narratives. To help define these six topics (i.e., combinations of structural leadership situation cues), we examined the top words and narratives (based on topic probabilities that represent the prevalence of topics within the narratives and words within the topics) for each topic, which can be found in online Supplemental Material F. Table 5 defines these six topics or combinations of structural situation cues: Market/Business Needs, Barriers to Effectiveness, Interpersonal Resources, Deviations/Changes, Team Objectives, and Logistics. Each topic/structural leadership situation cue combination captures individual structural situation cues such as who is typically in the situation, what (e.g., task) is typically happening in the situation, and where (e.g., industry) are these situations typically taking place. For instance, the structural situation cue combination Team Objectives reflect the people (e.g., subordinates) and tasks related to a leader establishing team member roles, delegating tasks, and building or maintaining collaboration. Meanwhile, Deviations/Changes highlight work environments, like restaurants and retail stores, where crowded restaurants or shops could result in tight timeframes and overwhelmed staff. Unlike Deviation/Changes that are characterized by more people present, Barriers to Effectiveness tend to include fewer people, including only those people (e.g., subordinate, client) directly involved in the confrontation or resolution.

Although the structural leadership situation cue combinations reflect "types" of leadership situations, a leader's situation can reflect every type simultaneously, albeit to varying extents. Structural topic modeling provides the proportion for which each leader's situation narrative is reflective of each structural leadership situation cue combination. Thus, every situation narrative has six structural scores (i.e., six proportions), each of which reflects one of the six structural leadership situation cue combinations. A situational narrative that is primarily reflective of one particular cue combination has a high structural score for that cue combination, but it nonetheless also has structural scores for the other five cue combinations (although these other scores are much lower).

Using structural topic modeling, we also examined the difference in topic proportions (e.g., how much a situation is associated with a topic) across groups, which is parallel to tests for scale score differences (e.g., how much a situation is characterized by a dimension) between groups. Specifically, we looked at differences in topic proportions for (a) hierarchical level of leader (frontline vs. mid-level vs. senior-level), (b) situation condition (momentary vs. chronic), and (c) leader gender (men vs. women). Online Supplemental Material F contains a summary of our results.

sections.header_raw: Relationship Between Psychological Leadership Situation Characteristics and Structural Leadership Situation Cues
sections.header_norm: relationship between psychological leadership situation characteristics and structural leadership situation cues
paragraphs: Examining the relationship between psychological leadership situation characteristics (via psychological leadership situation dimensions in the LSQ) and structural leadership situation cues (via structural leadership situation cue combinations) provides insight into the structural situation cue combinations that afford psychological characteristics (Rauthmann et al., 2014;Zaccaro et al., 2018). Features of the "objective" situation (like who, what, where, when) can create a context or provide "opportunities for action" that cause or allow the leader to perceive the situation in particular ways (as reflected by the psychological situation dimensions). For example, a task that requires the leader's team to build a marketing plan for a new product may provide affordances that the leader uses to infer excitement and creativity in the situation. Examining the relationship between the structural situation cue combinations and the psychological situation dimensions would help us understand if leaders are using these structural cues to infer the psychological characteristics of the situation (Asendorpf, 2015). As can be seen in Figure 1, the relationship between structural leadership situation cues and psychological leadership situation characteristics is expected to be mediated by leader information processing. Thus, the magnitude of the correlations between structural leadership situation cue combinations and dimensions of psychological leadership situation characteristics (i.e., the product of Paths "a" and "b" in Figure 1) is expected to be weak-to-moderate rather than strong.

Every leader in the sample provided both a narrative and adjective ratings; thus, every leader's situation has six topic scores (i.e., the proportion of the situation that belongs to each topic/structural situation cue combination as calculated via the topic modeling approach) and six LSQ scale scores (i.e., the situation's standing on each psychological situation dimension). By correlating the topic and LSQ scores for each leader, we examined the relationship between structural cue combinations and psychological dimensions of leadership situations. Table 4 contains the correlations between the topic scores and the LSQ scores for the validation sample (from the CFA, n = 407).foot_9 These correlations provide initial insight into the structural situation cue combinations that afford leaders' psychological perceptions of situations. For instance, the -.29 correlation between Barriers to Effectiveness and the Positive Uniqueness dimension suggests, as might be expected, that situations that contain problematic structural aspects (e.g., a confrontation between or with a subordinate) are perceived as less unique in a positive sense. Conversely, the .26 correlation between Team Objectives and the Positive Uniqueness dimension suggests that situations that have team-focused structural aspects (e.g., situations that involve subordinates) are perceived as more unique in a positive sense. Furthermore, the correlation between Team Objectives and the Scope dimension (r = .29) suggests that overseeing teams can cue leaders to focus on larger scope situation characteristics, perhaps because This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. there is a diversity of requirements (e.g., team members have different functions and/or are spread out across various locations) or perspectives. These weak-to-moderate strength correlations show that certain structural cue combinations afford psychological characteristics. The strength of these correlations was expected due to the fact that structural leadership situation cues are distal rather than proximal antecedents to psychological situation characteristics (see Figure 1). In addition, however, assessing psychological leadership situation characteristics via conventional self-report and structural leadership situation cues via Structural Topic Modeling (a machine learning approach) helped achieve realistic-strength (vs. artifactually high) correlations, as may be seen with same-source human ratings that may suffer from common-method variance. The lack of significant correlations between the structural situation cue combinations and the psychological situation dimension of Ease could be due in part to an unaccounted-for moderator: leader individual differences. The conceptual model of situations (see Figure 1) proposes that leader individual differences moderate the impact of structural situation cues on psychological situation characteristics. Thus, structural situation cue combinations may not afford perceptions of Ease unless the tasks, people, and environment align with a leader's knowledge, skills, abilities, and other characteristics (KSAOs). A leader may not interpret the situation as easy or simple unless he or she has the knowledge and skills necessary to handle such a situation.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
sections.header_raw: Relationship Between Psychological Leadership Situation Characteristics and Leader Behavior and Outcomes
sections.header_norm: relationship between psychological leadership situation characteristics and leader behavior and outcomes
paragraphs: In this section, we examine the relationships between the psychological leadership situation dimensions (as measured by the LSQ) and the leader's behavior and outcome in the situation.foot_10 These relationships reflect Paths "d" and "f," respectively, in Figure 1. The present study assessed leadership behavior in the situation and situational outcomes via leaders' narratives within the survey. Specifically, in addition to describing their situation using an open-ended question (which was used to analyze the structural leadership situation cues) and in addition to rating the situation using a list of adjectives (which was used to create the LSQ, measuring psychological leadership situation characteristics), leaders also separately described their behavior in that situation and the results (outcomes) of the situation using two additional open-ended questions. Because the outcome of a leadership situation is easier to conceptualize for momentary than for chronic situations, we focused this analysis on momentary leadership situations.

These initial empirical tests were necessarily exploratory: The specific composition of the psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy was necessarily unknown at the inception of the present research project because the taxonomy was developed through empirical means. Accordingly, we interpret our results based on plausibility and conceptual connections to existing leadership research.

Using automated coding analyses, each behavior narrative was scored for initiating structure and consideration behaviors. Initiating structure and consideration were represented via the initiating structure and consideration subscales, respectively, from the Leader Behavior Description Questionnaire (LBDQ-XII; Stogdill, 1963). These subscales were used to train a model in Python using the Flair library (Akbik et al., 2019), which was used to provide each leader's behavior narrative two scores, one each on initiating structure and consideration, where higher scores equate to a higher presence of a given type of leadership behavior. To check our model's automated scoring, 20 behaviors the model scored as high in initiating structure or high in consideration were coded by a coauthor of this article as being representative of either initiating structure or consideration. Upon finding agreement to be acceptable (90%), we continued with the analysis. Online Supplemental Material G provides more details about the training of models and the agreement check.

Similarly, we assigned leaders' results narratives valence scores to represent whether the outcomes of the situation were positive or negative. We did so because conceptualizations of situational outcomes in terms of positive and negative valence are common in psychology (see, e.g., Vroom, 1964) and because existing measures of leadership effectiveness in particular often reduce effectiveness to a continuum from negative (ineffective) to positive (effective) valence (e.g., Simon et al., 2022;van Knippenberg & van Knippenberg, 2005). A pre-trained sentiment analysis model from the Python library Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020) was used to score each leader's results narrative on positivity and negativity. To provide a single outcome score for each narrative, we divided the positive sentiment score by the negative sentiment score, such that higher outcome valence scores indicated more positive outcomes in the situation. Please see online Supplemental Material G for more details on sentiment scoring.

Table 6 presents the correlations between psychological leadership situation dimensions (as measured by the LSQ) and leader behavior (initiating structure and consideration) as well as outcome valence. The psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy measured by the LSQ was not built on an explicit theory of behavior-or outcome-relevance to situations. Nonetheless, the direction (i.e., sign) of many correlations seemed in line with previous leadership research or else otherwise reasonable. For instance, situations high (vs. low) in Ease, Typicality, and Positive Uniqueness were associated with more positive outcome valence. As noted previously, these three psychological leadership situation dimensions measured by the LSQ are associated with the dimension of familiar versus novel events in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy. The results can therefore be interpreted in light of Hoffman and Lord's speculations: Specifically, situations high in Ease and Typicality, which relate to Hoffman and Lord's familiarity, "can be quickly and efficiently matched to learned patterns" via a decision-making process known as recognition primed decision-making; conversely, situations high in Positive Uniqueness, which relate to novelty, involve creativity or innovation and "may be more effective in explorative contexts" (p. 561). Indirect support for this last contention also comes from Podsakoff et al. (1996), whose meta-analysis revealed a positive relationship between task intrinsic satisfaction (related to exciting, special tasks) and the outcome of employee satisfaction.

Additionally, our results indicated that situations high (vs. low) in Importance were associated with more initiating structure behavior, which is consistent with Vroom's (2000) finding that leaders seek less subordinate involvement for important decisions (even though, This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

normatively, they should seek more subordinate involvement for such decisions). Our findings that situations high (vs. low) in Scope were associated with more initiating structure behavior and better outcomes are peripherally related to meta-analytic findings involving positive relationships between team size and leader initiating structure (Mullen et al., 1989) and between organization size and productivity (Gooding & Wagner, 1985).

In this section, we examine the relationships between the psychological leadership situation dimensions (as measured by the LSQ) and the leader's behavior and outcome in the situation.foot_10 These relationships reflect Paths "d" and "f," respectively, in Figure 1. The present study assessed leadership behavior in the situation and situational outcomes via leaders' narratives within the survey. Specifically, in addition to describing their situation using an open-ended question (which was used to analyze the structural leadership situation cues) and in addition to rating the situation using a list of adjectives (which was used to create the LSQ, measuring psychological leadership situation characteristics), leaders also separately described their behavior in that situation and the results (outcomes) of the situation using two additional open-ended questions. Because the outcome of a leadership situation is easier to conceptualize for momentary than for chronic situations, we focused this analysis on momentary leadership situations.

These initial empirical tests were necessarily exploratory: The specific composition of the psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy was necessarily unknown at the inception of the present research project because the taxonomy was developed through empirical means. Accordingly, we interpret our results based on plausibility and conceptual connections to existing leadership research.

Using automated coding analyses, each behavior narrative was scored for initiating structure and consideration behaviors. Initiating structure and consideration were represented via the initiating structure and consideration subscales, respectively, from the Leader Behavior Description Questionnaire (LBDQ-XII; Stogdill, 1963). These subscales were used to train a model in Python using the Flair library (Akbik et al., 2019), which was used to provide each leader's behavior narrative two scores, one each on initiating structure and consideration, where higher scores equate to a higher presence of a given type of leadership behavior. To check our model's automated scoring, 20 behaviors the model scored as high in initiating structure or high in consideration were coded by a coauthor of this article as being representative of either initiating structure or consideration. Upon finding agreement to be acceptable (90%), we continued with the analysis. Online Supplemental Material G provides more details about the training of models and the agreement check.

Similarly, we assigned leaders' results narratives valence scores to represent whether the outcomes of the situation were positive or negative. We did so because conceptualizations of situational outcomes in terms of positive and negative valence are common in psychology (see, e.g., Vroom, 1964) and because existing measures of leadership effectiveness in particular often reduce effectiveness to a continuum from negative (ineffective) to positive (effective) valence (e.g., Simon et al., 2022;van Knippenberg & van Knippenberg, 2005). A pre-trained sentiment analysis model from the Python library Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020) was used to score each leader's results narrative on positivity and negativity. To provide a single outcome score for each narrative, we divided the positive sentiment score by the negative sentiment score, such that higher outcome valence scores indicated more positive outcomes in the situation. Please see online Supplemental Material G for more details on sentiment scoring.

Table 6 presents the correlations between psychological leadership situation dimensions (as measured by the LSQ) and leader behavior (initiating structure and consideration) as well as outcome valence. The psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy measured by the LSQ was not built on an explicit theory of behavior-or outcome-relevance to situations. Nonetheless, the direction (i.e., sign) of many correlations seemed in line with previous leadership research or else otherwise reasonable. For instance, situations high (vs. low) in Ease, Typicality, and Positive Uniqueness were associated with more positive outcome valence. As noted previously, these three psychological leadership situation dimensions measured by the LSQ are associated with the dimension of familiar versus novel events in Hoffman and Lord's (2013) conceptual taxonomy. The results can therefore be interpreted in light of Hoffman and Lord's speculations: Specifically, situations high in Ease and Typicality, which relate to Hoffman and Lord's familiarity, "can be quickly and efficiently matched to learned patterns" via a decision-making process known as recognition primed decision-making; conversely, situations high in Positive Uniqueness, which relate to novelty, involve creativity or innovation and "may be more effective in explorative contexts" (p. 561). Indirect support for this last contention also comes from Podsakoff et al. sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: This empirical taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics includes six psychological dimensions (Positive Uniqueness, Importance, Negativity, Scope, Typicality, and Ease) that collectively define a leader's perceptions of the underlying qualities of the objects and actors at work. The objects and actors themselves (as opposed to a leader's perceptions thereof) are summarized by the present study's six structural leadership situation cue combinations (Market/Business Needs, Barriers to Effectiveness, Interpersonal Resources, Deviations/Changes, Team Objectives, Logistics).

Accompanying the taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics, we developed a self-report measure, the LSQ, and presented three forms of validity evidence. First, the lexical approach was used to create the taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and subsequently the LSQ, providing content validity. Specifically, the adjectives within the LSQ were empirically sourced from a corpus capturing a broad list of descriptors related to leadership situations. Second, CFA results on a validation (holdout) sample of leader situation ratings (using only adjectives in the LSQ measure) supported the dimensions of psychological leadership situation characteristics revealed in the EFA, thus providing construct validity. Finally, the LSQ's relationships with leader behavior and outcomes appear consistent with relevant prior research, providing initial criterion-related validity evidence, although we note that these relationships are based on cross-sectional correlations.

The present study's empirically derived taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics provided a valid and sufficient taxonomy with which conceptual leadership situation taxonomies (Hoffman & Lord, 2013) and empirical general (non-leadership-specific) situation taxonomies (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014) could be compared. Several dimensions in our taxonomy overlapped with empirical general situation taxonomies and Hoffman and Lord's conceptual leadership situation taxonomy, as summarized in Table 3. Yet, within this overlap, we also found differences related to connotation (where our empirical leadership-focused taxonomy included more functional rather than emotional/affective terms than the empirical general situation taxonomies), polarity (where our empirical leadership-focused taxonomy revealed unipolar dimensions, such as those involving positive and negative situations, rather than single bipolar dimensions posited by a conceptual leadershipfocused taxonomy), and mismatches in conceptual coverage (where our empirical leadership-focused taxonomy revealed new groupings/ dimensions of content compared to a conceptual leadership-focused taxonomy). Furthermore, our taxonomy omitted dimensions from other taxonomies (due to relevance/applicability to leadership) and introduced a new dimension of Scope, which, as we noted previously, seems particularly relevant to leadership (vs. general) situations. These comparisons provided a comprehensive empirical test (emphasizing both confirmation and disconfirmation) of a previous conceptual leadership taxonomy and demonstrated the contribution of an empirical taxonomy developed specifically within the leadership context over and above existing general situation taxonomies.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: The taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and the typology of structural leadership situation cues provide the foundation for researchers to, over time, develop propositions and test pieces of the conceptual model of leadership situations, as summarized in Figure 1. The present study itself included initial tests of portions of this nomological network. Specifically, we examined the associations of the psychological leadership situation characteristics (as measured by the LSQ) with the structural leadership situation cue combinations, leader personality, leader behavior (initiating structure and consideration), and leadership situation outcome valence (positivity vs. negativity of outcomes). Results from these analyses largely aligned with intuitive expectations and with previous leadership theories and empirical research. Thus, our taxonomy provides the basis for developing testable propositions about the role of the situation in leadership research.

Our psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy, derived through a lexical approach, exhibits what Cooper and Richardson (1986) refer to as "procedural equivalence," meaning that the various dimensions were developed and measured (via the LSQ) "with equal fidelity" (p. 179). We did not privilege one leadership theory over another: Our empirical taxonomy started from first principles and was "blind" (at least until it came to labeling and defining the factors that had already been uncovered) to specific leadership theories. Thus, our taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics makes it possible to evaluate and compare existing leadership theories, note gaps in these theories, and build new leadership theories, thereby furthering leadership research on situations.

Specifically, as shown in Table 3, this taxonomy effort provides a common framework by which to reveal the situational coverage (vs. omissions) of various situation-based leadership theories. Some theories consider several psychological leadership situation characteristics but within theoretical frameworks that narrowly limit This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. these characteristics by tying them to a small number of structural leadership situation cues. An example is Kerr and Jermier's (1978) substitutes for leadership model that includes situational characteristics like task intrinsic satisfaction (which conceptually exists within our Positive Uniqueness dimension), group cohesion (conceptually within our Negativity dimension), and task routineness (conceptually within our Typicality dimension). However, our dimensions of, for instance, Positive Uniqueness and Typicality conceptually cover more than just characteristics of the task: They can also cover characteristics of teams and organizations, as studied by previous leadership theories, as well as structural leadership situation cue combinations, as presented by our structural typology. By emphasizing a comprehensive set of psychological leadership situation characteristics (and their mapping onto a comprehensive set of structural leadership situation cue combinations), as opposed to the unnecessarily narrow sets of structural cue-psychological characteristic combinations previously emphasized in the leadership literature (e.g., "task complexity," "subordinate trust"), the present taxonomy allows for the generalization of past situation-based leadership theories to a much larger and better defined set of situation characteristics.

In a related vein, our taxonomy facilitates the comparison of situations across leadership studies, particularly studies of leadership behavioral styles (e.g., transformational leadership). The lack of core situational dimensions that apply across the range of leadership behavioral styles has limited the situational focus of these theories. The present research offers core psychological and structural characteristics that can be used to summarize even the most specific and idiosyncratic situation characteristics examined by previous leadership studies. This framework by which to compare situations across studies will broaden the situations examined in previous metaanalyses (particularly those related to leadership behavioral styles) and advance the study of situations in leadership theories.

In addition to organizing existing situational leadership efforts within a common framework, the present study's taxonomy enables future situational leadership efforts by identifying gaps in existing research. For instance, our taxonomy's Scope dimension is uncommonly identified in, and therefore understudied by, existing leadership research. Future research that uses our measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics, the LSQ, would be able to measure all six psychological dimensions in a single study-a comprehensive and sufficient approach to the study of psychological leadership situation characteristics.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: The present article also has important practical implications. Any perusal of practitioner-focused writings suggests that leadership practitioners are well aware of the power of the situation. Thus far, however, practitioners have had to confront a leadership research literature whose focus on situations has been scattered across numerous leadership theories and empirical works-and that has unnecessarily yoked specific structural cues to specific psychological characteristics. Through its examination of the relationships between the structural cues and psychological characteristics of leadership situations, the present research helps organizations understand how leaders perceive and interpret leadership challenges (see also, in this regard, our subsequent discussion of situational judgment tests and assessment centers). Moreover, the present research sets the stage for a better understanding of the predictive power of perceived leadership situations-that is, the impact of perceived leadership situations on leader behavior and leadership outcomes of situations-thereby benefiting practitioners as they attract, select, assess, and develop leaders and as they create contexts that facilitate leader effectiveness.

For example, an understanding of the psychological leadership situation characteristics for a specific leadership job would contribute to organizational efforts to attract and select leaders to that job based on person-job fit. If this is done for a sufficient number of leadership jobs, such jobs could be grouped (e.g., through cluster analysis) in terms of the similarity of their psychological situation dimension scores. Doing so would help organizations identify underlying differences between putatively similar jobs as well as underlying similarities across putatively dissimilar jobs (e.g., those with different job titles)-therefore contributing to the discussion about the likely success or failure of leaders who move to seemingly very different jobs (e.g., a senior-level politician who subsequently becomes the president of a large university). As another example, an understanding of the impact of cognitively oriented individual differences (i.e., KSAOs) on psychological leadership situation characteristics would aid in leadership selection efforts as well as in the identification of specific areas for leader development. As a third example, an understanding of the relationships between structural leadership situation cues and psychological leadership situation characteristics would aid in job redesign efforts aimed at improving the situation cues associated with a specific leadership jobthereby optimizing the psychological situation characteristics for leader effectiveness.

As a third example, an understanding of the relationships between structural leadership situation cues and psychological leadership situation characteristics would aid in job redesign efforts aimed at improving the situation cues associated with a specific leadership jobthereby optimizing the psychological situation characteristics for leader effectiveness.
sections.preview_head: The present article also has important practical implications. Any perusal of practitioner-focused writings suggests that leadership practitioners are well aware of the power of the situation. Thus far, however, practitioners have had to confront a leadership research literature whose focus on situations has been scattered across numerous leadership theories and empirical works-and that has unnecessarily yoked specific structural cues to specific psychological characteristics.

paragraphs: Our study was by no means immune to limitations. First, although we provided comparisons between the present taxonomy, on the one hand, and both general situation taxonomies and the situation characteristics proposed by leadership theories, on the other hand, these comparisons are themselves conceptual rather than empirical. Future research should, therefore, empirically test the conceptual overlap between the present psychological leadership situation characteristics (as measured by the LSQ) and the situation characteristics proposed by situation-based leadership theories. For instance, leaders could be asked to rate situation characteristics like task complexity and team interdependence (House, 1996;Melcher, 1979) in addition to completing the LSQ, so as to facilitate empirical comparisons. Future research should also empirically compare the present psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy to general situation taxonomies (e.g., Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014) by asking leaders to rate situations using the LSQ and measures of these general situation taxonomies.

A second limitation was that although the present study provided preliminary validity evidence by examining the relationship of LSQ scores with measures of leader behavior and leadership situation outcomes, further validation efforts are clearly needed. Despite the likelihood of common-method variance inflating relationships, it would be beneficial for future research to re-examine situationbehavior relationships using a conventional self-report measure of leader initiating structure and consideration such as the LBDQ-XII (Stogdill, 1963) to complement the present approach, which used the LBDQ-XII only to train a Python automated coding model (i.e., Flair; Akbik et al., 2019). This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

A third limitation was our primary use of online panel samples from MTurk and Qualtrics. Future research should replicate the dimensional structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics on non-panel samples. 13 Nonetheless, we are aware of no a priori reason why the measurement invariance (i.e., equivalence) observed in the panel samples should not generalize to non-panel samples. Our psychological leadership situation characteristics taxonomy exhibits what Cooper and Richardson (1986) refer to as "distributional equivalence," meaning that the scores associated with the various dimensions "vary over equivalent ranges of values in their respective populations" (p. 179), likely because: (a) our participants were leaders from a variety of occupations, organizations, and levels, (b) we deliberately assessed both momentary and chronic leadership situations experienced by our participants, (c) our participants themselves varied in terms of gender and (though not an explicit focus of our article) several other demographic characteristics, and (d) the structure of the taxonomy generalized (i.e., was invariant across) leader hierarchical levels, momentary versus chronic situations, and leader gender. Thus, the settings and samples are likely to facilitate appreciable variability in a variety of situational characteristics (and outcomes thereof). However, the structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics should be fully replicated on a sample of leaders not derived from online participant panels.

Ideally, such samples could also be collected from countries that differ from the United States along cultural dimensions (e.g., Den Hartog et al., 1999;Hofstede, 1998) that have the potential to influence leader situation perceptions. Furthermore, related to the leaders sampled, this taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics focuses on formal leadership, as defined by three tiers of leadership-frontline, mid-level, and senior-level-where we equate leadership to those who formally supervise at least one subordinate. Beyond these three tiers of leaders, however, there are informal or not-yet-formal leaders, such as emerging and aspiring leaders. Future research should examine whether the presently obtained structure of psychological leadership situation characteristics applies equally to these quasi-leadership roles.

Additionally, it is important to note that variability in psychological leadership situation characteristic scores is attributable to underlying differences in both the structural situations that are interpreted psychologically by human raters and the idiosyncratic perceptions of each rater. In this regard, a fourth limitation is that, although the present study did examine relationships of psychological leadership situation characteristics with both structural leadership cue combinations and leader personality, it did not aim to comprehensively disentangle the sources of variance in psychological leadership situation characteristics. Future research could aim to do so, in part by assessing how various structural situation cue combinations from our typology (e.g., via a series of standardized video vignettes) are interpreted psychologically by people in those situations-and the extent to which ratings of the psychological characteristics might vary not just across the structural situations themselves but also across the roles (e.g., leaders vs. subordinates) as well as within the roles (e.g., one leader vs. another) of people experiencing (or, in this case, observing) these structural situations as a function of these people's dispositional characteristics.

Although psychological situation characteristics are likely to exert a greater influence on leader behavior than structural situation cues (Mischel et al., 2002), a fifth limitation of the present study was that we developed a survey measure of only the psychological leadership situation characteristics. We did not develop a corresponding survey measure of the structural leadership situation cue combinations. At one level, our analysis of structural situation cues in the present research went beyond their traditional treatment in previous general situation taxonomy development efforts (e.g., Rauthmann et al., 2014). Specifically, topic modeling of leaders' situation narratives provided a holistic picture of the types of structural situation cue combinations faced by leaders. Nonetheless, to further our understanding of the relationship between structural leadership situation cues and psychological leadership situation characteristics-in other words, the ways in which structural situation cue combinations afford psychological situation perceptionsfuture research is necessary to develop a measure of structural leadership situation cue combinations. One way to develop a survey measure would be to create a checklist of structural cues (e.g., number and types of people present, environment, tasks), where combinations or patterns of these cues within the checklist could be mapped to the structural leadership situation cue combinations that compose our typology.

sections.paper_id: Green et al. - 2023 - An empirical taxonomy of leadership situations Development, validation, and implications for the sc
paragraphs: Beyond the future research directions proposed previously-in the context of addressing limitations of the present researchadditional future research directions are readily apparent. We focus on articulating three broad "directions" (themes) for future research. Within each direction, we provide several specific future research questions.

Previous empirical leadership research often focuses on chronic situations, whereas the study of "event"-type situations requires a focus on momentary situations. The LSQ provides an important vehicle for the study of momentary psychological leadership situation characteristics via ecological momentary assessments (Beal, 2015), in which leadership situations are assessed over multiple time points. An important topic in the study of momentary situations involves temporal trajectories of situations (Dalal et al., 2020). For instance, the perceived Typicality of leadership situations may increase gradually over long timeframes (months or years) as the leader's job tenure increases. Conversely, perceived Typicality may drop sharply upon the advent of a global pandemic such as COVID-19 or, more prosaically, a new subordinate. Thus, the first direction for future research is the study of temporal trajectories of perceived leadership situation characteristics.

Recently, the general (non-leadership) literature has focused on not just momentary situations (Parrigon et al., 2017;Rauthmann et al., 2014) but also their relationships with personality states (Dalal et al., 2015(Dalal et al., , 2020;;Sosnowska et al., 2019). The paucity of research in the leadership literature on these "systems"-type interactions (Buss, 1977;Zuroff, 1986)-that is, interactions involving dynamic, reciprocal "transactions" (Rauthmann & Sherman, 2020) between the situation and the leader-represents a major opportunity for leadership This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

researchers (as also noted by Dinh & Lord, 2012) and, therefore, a second direction for future research. Such relationships, too, can now be empirically tested via the LSQ. Specifically, such studies would involve the examination of reciprocal relationships between the leader and the situation, where leader personality states and leader situation perceptions are measured at various points in time (for similar studies, albeit using nonleader samples, see Green et al., 2019;Judge et al., 2014). Such studies could therefore examine how present leader personality states and present leader situation perceptions (at time t) influence future leader personality states and future leader situation perceptions (at time t + 1 and beyond). Examination of causal direction between situation perceptions and personality states may reveal the extent to which perceived situation characteristics call for certain leader personality states, or, conversely, the extent to which leader personality states result in perceived situation characteristics through leaders' attempts to choose, shape, and interpret situations (Dalal et al., 2015). Finally, leader constancy in situation perceptions or, conversely, leader responsiveness to situations, if treated as a leader attribute (i.e., individual difference variable), could be studied as a moderator of the relationship between leader situation perceptions and leader behavior (Zaccaro et al., 2018).

Other types of studies to measure reciprocal relationships between the leader and the situation could involve situational judgment tests or assessment centers. This third direction for future research could be of particular benefit to practitioners interested in using situational judgment tests or assessment centers in leadership assessment and/or development contexts. Specifically, to reflect the full range of leadership situations, the scenarios (in situational judgment tests) or exercises (in assessment centers) should be designed to reflect all six structural situation cue combinations and all six dimensions of psychological situation characteristics uncovered by the present research. These assessments could then measure leaders' likelihood of entering these situations and their resulting behavior, and whether these actions are related to particular leader KSAOs. Understanding such KSAO-situation relationships would help researchers and practitioners alike when developing new and evaluating present assessments. For instance, if leaders were to rate the psychological leadership situation characteristics of situational judgment tests or assessment centers, researchers could assess: (a) the extent of leader interrater agreement regarding the psychological situation characteristics associated with the same structural situation cue combinations, (b) how leader KSAOs interact with structural situation cue combinations to determine psychological situation characteristic ratings, and (c) how psychological situation characteristics mediate the relationship between structural situation cue combinations and leader behavior.

paragraphs: The present study's empirical taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and typology of structural leadership situation cue combinations, as well as the resulting survey measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics (the LSQ), contributes to the literature by organizing and summarizing existing leadership situation research and conceptual taxonomies as well as by providing a foundation for future leadership situation research and practice.
sections.text: The present study's empirical taxonomy of psychological leadership situation characteristics and typology of structural leadership situation cue combinations, as well as the resulting survey measure of psychological leadership situation characteristics (the LSQ), contributes to the literature by organizing and summarizing existing leadership situation research and conceptual taxonomies as well as by providing a foundation for future leadership situation research and practice.
